articles.title: The Thrift Economy
articles.subtitle: The Find, the Floor, and What Comes After
articles.author: Arjun Mehta
articles.lead: Sarojini Nagar on a Saturday has always been about knowing something the next person doesn't. What changed is who's in on it — and what they're doing with what they learn.

body.text: The pile doesn't wait for you. Get there after ten and the cargo pants are gone, the Dickies jacket in the right shade of khaki has been pocketed by someone who got there at nine-thirty, and you're left negotiating over things you don't actually want. This is the first discipline of Sarojini Nagar: timing. The second is eye. The third is knowing when to walk.

India's second-hand apparel market is worth over three billion dollars and growing at thirteen percent annually. That number lives in a report somewhere. What it actually represents is a few hundred thousand people in Indian metros who have built a fluency in fashion that didn't exist a decade ago — not from studying it, but from being inside the pile every weekend since they were seventeen.
We're not anti-consumption. We're anti-stupidity. Why pay eight thousand for something fast-fashion when you can find something better for four hundred and have a story to go with it?
The Account as Medium

The resale account changed the topology of Indian fashion more than any designer of the last ten years. On Instagram, the thrift page operates as a micro-boutique with its own editorial logic — curated drops, story-format reveals, DM-to-close transactions, waitlists that are real. Accounts like @archives_heaven work the high-low fluency that this generation has in its bones: the Huda liner, the export-surplus jacket, the Coach bag bought for eight hundred from someone in Bengaluru who bought it for three hundred from a stall in Bandra. Every item carrying a provenance story that a branded label can never manufacture.

The labels understand this. Why do you think every D2C brand launched since 2021 is cosplaying editorial? They watched the thrift accounts build genuine communities around the act of selection and realised that the selection was the product, not the garment. The thrift account discovered this first.
The Tension Nobody Talks About

Sarojini Nagar was never 'thrifting' for the people who couldn't afford to shop anywhere else. It was just shopping. The arrival of a generation that reframed it as conscious consumption — that brought the GRWM camera, that applied the language of capsule drops to a pile of export surplus — changed the market. Prices went up in the good sections. The finding got harder because more people were looking for the same things. Mangaldas in Mumbai, Commercial Street in Bengaluru, Moore Market in Chennai — same pattern, different city.

The strategic thrift shopper has arrived with a global fashion education the vendor didn't anticipate. That education is real and has made the market measurably more competitive and slightly less accessible to the person it originally served. This is not an accusation. It is a fact about who benefits from aesthetic revolutions — and it is the fact that anyone doing serious work in this space has to sit with.
Thrifting used to be something you did if you couldn't afford better. Now it's the thing you do to prove you know better. That shift has consequences.
What the Find Produces

The end of this story is not better thrift. The people who came up through the pile and understood it most clearly are now making things. MURMUR in Delhi started buying surplus from Noida garment factories — not for thrift, but to use the constraint as a design brief. NEEDLE in Chennai applied the collector logic of the limited-run zine to garment production: numbered editions, no restocking, the scarcity that comes from the production method rather than a marketing team.

This is what the thrift economy actually produced: a generation that learned to think about fashion as a resource problem with creative solutions. The ones doing it most honestly are not trying to scale. They are trying to stay specific. That specificity is the thing the market cannot replicate, which is precisely why it's worth doing.

articles.title: Floors Without Walls
articles.subtitle: How India's underground built itself from nothing — and what it built
articles.author: Priya Nair
articles.lead: Madhav Shorey was studying graphic design in Melbourne when he started sketching out what a label from Delhi could look like. He was thinking about Mughal architecture. He named it Qilla — fort, in Urdu. He'd been going to the Red Fort since he was a kid.

body.text: That detail matters. The Delhi scene that built the infrastructure for India's current underground was not built by people who came from event management or from money. It was built by a drummer who turned to electronics because his bandmates kept missing rehearsal, a graphic designer who wanted a platform for music he believed in, a network of people who were just trying to hear something different in a city that wasn't offering it.

Qilla Records launched in 2011 with a dark, fragmented techno EP by Jitter. Madhav describes him as 'easily one of my favourite producers and DJs, and he was really ahead of his time. No one knew what the hell this guy was playing.' The label's 100th release, Chakravyūh — a 35-track compilation spanning 12 countries, its first vinyl pressing — came in 2024. Thirteen years between those two moments. The vinyl had to be pressed in Europe, stored in Europe, shipped from Europe: 'We have to pay VAT above cost price, which we cannot claim back as an Indian outfit. The heavy import duties don't allow us to bring our stock back into India either.' The economics make no sense. They did it anyway.
The MO of the day was to DIY and figure it out. We really planted the seeds back then. There was not a single club playing electronic music — it was just everything else.
The People Who Built It

The scene that exists today was built between roughly 2007 and 2015 by a specific cohort of people in NCR: Bass Foundation (Ed and Maarten), DJ Mocity at Boxout, Arjun Vagale and Dev Bhatia at Unmute, Munbir Chawla and Sarah at Wild City, BLOT! (Gaurav Malaker, who co-founded Qilla for its first two years), Vridian, Kohra himself. The venues were Elevate, Kuki, Manre, Ivy — none of which exist anymore. BLOT! ran Moar Disco from 2010. The Midival Punditz had Cyber Mehfil from 1997. Bhavishyavani Future Soundz in Mumbai was throwing drum and bass parties in 1999. The foundation went down earlier than people remember.

What this cohort did was not just build events. They built media (Wild City), distribution infrastructure (Boxout), a record label (Qilla), a booking and talent operation (Unmute). When people talk about 'the scene,' they mean the music. The scene is actually the infrastructure that made the music possible to hear.
Sanaya Ardeshir and the Live Question

Sanaya Ardeshir grew up in Jamshedpur — a steel plant town, her father an engineer who'd wanted to be a musician and wasn't in an India that didn't have the infrastructure for it. She started performing as Sandunes in 2012, developing a live set that integrated piano, synthesis, and production at a time when the Indian scene was almost entirely DJ-based. The Barbican commission from Warp and Boiler Room. The Richard Spaven tour. The !K7 release. She moved to Los Angeles in 2023.

That last fact — the move — is the data point the scene is most reluctant to discuss. The artists who build domestic credibility and international reach face a structural problem: the venues that can pay appropriately are not in India. The 1 AM curfew in Delhi. The sound system budgets that, as Kohra notes, represent 'not even 5% of what promoters will spend on a headliner.' The market that is growing fast and still cannot fully support the artists it's growing around.

We've gone from barely one major club per city to multiple clubs in not just the major metros but even in smaller cities. Audiences are more open now. They want to be challenged, not just entertained.
What the Floor Looks Like Now

The new collectives — Antariksh, ONNO Collective, Padmini Records — represent a generation that grew up watching this infrastructure get built and is now using it. Streaming data shows double-digit growth in techno and house playlist creation in India. The Magnetic Fields festival at Alsisar Mahal in Rajasthan books Rival Consoles and Barker alongside Vieux Farka Touré. DGTL landed in India. Block 22 in Hyderabad has hosted Mind Against, Adriatique, Sasha.

But the most honest version of the scene is still the one that doesn't have a name. The WhatsApp group that gets activated at 11 PM. The location pin that changes twice before you get there. The room you find at the end of it that is playing something you have never heard before, in a city that is figuring out it has one of the most interesting music scenes in the world, by people who mostly don't know that the world doesn't know it yet.

articles.title: No Logo Required
articles.subtitle: What it actually means to build a fashion label without the machinery
articles.author: Kabir Singh
articles.lead: There is a version of Indian homegrown fashion that is extremely good at describing itself. And then there are the labels that let the thing speak — the ones you find out about because someone whose taste you trust is wearing something you cannot place and won't explain.

body.text: The phrase 'homegrown Indian fashion label' has a specific set of associations now. The founding story about wanting to make something different. The Instagram drops timed to the algorithm. The influencer seed list. The collab reveal with a platform brand three years in. A price point that tests the upper limit of what 'accessible' can mean while maintaining aspirational distance. This is not a criticism — it is a description of a strategy that several labels have executed well against a 380-million-person GenZ market that was waiting to be addressed in its own register.

But the market it created has a shadow economy. Smaller, less visible, significantly more interesting. These are the labels that are not drop-scheduling, not building toward the collab reveal, not managing a seed list. Their marketing budget, as one designer put it without drama, is the product itself.
We don't do PR. We don't do influencer seeding. Our entire marketing budget is the product itself. If the product is right, the people who understand it will find it. They always have.
MURMUR, Delhi: The Constraint as Method

Delhi's garment industry produces more fabric than it uses. The Noida factories that supply the export market generate surplus constantly — end-of-roll stock, overruns from orders that changed spec, material that got written off before it became anything. MURMUR buys this material. Not as a sustainability statement, though the sustainability logic is real. As a design brief. The constraint of found material forces choices that a blank canvas doesn't demand. When you cannot choose the fabric, you start making other choices with more precision: the silhouette, the proportion, the construction logic that has to work with what you have.

The pieces are priced between eighteen hundred and twelve thousand rupees. That is not cheap. It is not trying to be cheap. It is trying to compete on specificity — on the fact that the piece in your hands is the product of a specific material constraint that cannot be repeated because that bolt of fabric is gone. This is not limited edition as marketing device. It is limited edition as honest description of the production process.
NEEDLE, Chennai: The Zine Logic Applied to Clothing

Arun Kumar came to garment-making from graphic design, through screen printing. He started NEEDLE by applying the logic he already understood: the zine model. A zine with 150 copies is not a limited edition. It is the amount that existed. NEEDLE labels each piece with its run number — sewn in, not printed, permanent. A piece marked 034/150 is the 34th of 150 made. When the run is gone it is gone. There is no restock. This is not a policy. It is a consequence of the production method.

The relationship this creates between the object and the person who ends up with it is categorically different from the relationship created by a numbered-edition drop with a waitlist managed by a Shopify plugin. The scarcity is structural, not manufactured. The number is a fact about the piece's existence, not a marketing lever. Collectors — and these pieces attract collectors — understand the difference immediately.
The numbered edition model came from zine culture. A zine with 150 copies isn't a limited edition. It's just the amount that existed. We apply the same logic to a shirt.
The Question of Scale, and Why It Is the Wrong Question

The obvious critique of what MURMUR and NEEDLE are doing is that it doesn't scale. MURMUR cannot become a company with a thousand SKUs. NEEDLE cannot open a national retail operation. The model that produces interesting things is the model that prevents you from building what investors want to fund and trade journalists want to write profiles about. This is understood by the people running these labels and is not experienced as a problem.

Several of these labels have operated for three to five years without external funding and without the visibility that would attract it. In a D2C landscape with brutal attrition rates, that's a data point worth taking seriously. The model they are running is not optimizing for the trajectory the broader market is following. It is optimizing for the quality of the object and the integrity of the relationship between object and owner. Whether that is viable long-term is a question. Whether it is interesting right now is not.

articles.title: The Psy-Trance Revival
articles.subtitle: India reclaims the genre it gave the world and didn't get to keep
articles.author: Ananya Rao
articles.lead: In Kohra and SHFT's 'Morjim Mystery' — on Qilla's hundredth release — you can hear something the genre's founders never intended to put there: an Indian producer sampling Goa trance in Goa, from inside it, knowing exactly what they're doing.

body.text: Psytrance was made in India by people who were not Indian, for an audience that was largely not Indian, using spiritual imagery from a culture whose people were not invited to the dancefloor. This is not contested history — Arun Saldanha's academic work on the Anjuna scene documents the racial topology of those early parties with precision. The local villagers were outside, selling chai. The Goa trance dancefloor was a colonial leisure structure dressed up in Indian aesthetics. The genre it produced became one of the most globally influential in electronic music.

It also became, in India, a source of cultural embarrassment for a long time. The local electronic scene that developed through the 2000s and 2010s — the scene that Kohra, BLOT!, Sandunes, and Arjun Vagale built in Delhi, Mumbai, and Bengaluru — largely distanced itself from the psytrance legacy. The new Indian underground was techno, it was house, it was deep and minimal and globally literate. It was specifically not the thing tourists associated with Indian electronic music.
That music was made here and then it left. It went global and India stayed outside the story it generated. Now we're bringing it back — but this time from inside.
What the Sound Actually Is

Psytrance sits between 140 and 150 BPM, built over a low thick kick with prominent sub-bass. The genre's signature is the squelch: a sawtooth wave through a resonant filter, producing a sound that is organic and almost biological. A psytrance track is an exercise in patience — long intro, meditative build, the bass arriving, then several minutes later the material the track was always leading to. The sampling tradition is promiscuously syncretic: sitar phrases, Vedic chant fragments, dialogue from science fiction films, processed field recordings. The genre has always taken from everywhere and pretended the taking was reverence.

Infected Mushroom gave psytrance its first global mainstream moment in the early 2000s. Astrix, Vini Vici, and the Israeli acts that followed kept it alive through the period when its commercial peak had passed. Matsuri Productions' 1997 compilation 'Let It RIP' — the title both joke and farewell — marked the moment the first wave acknowledged the party was ending. What happened after is stranger: a genre that has been declared dead every five years and keeps not dying, finding new audiences in places its founders never imagined.
The Indian Producers Taking It Back

White Wizard, Farebi Jalebi, Braindrop, Silent Horror. These are the names of the contemporary Indian producers doing the most interesting work in the psytrance space. They are not making nostalgia music. They are taking the genre's vocabulary and running it through references that are specifically and unapologetically Indian — not the tourist's India of the Anjuna posters but the actual India that the people making this music grew up in.

When an Indian producer samples a Carnatic vocal phrase in a psytrance track, the relationship between that sample and the surrounding music is categorically different from what it was when a German producer sampled the same tradition in 1993. The knowledge is embodied differently. The context is inhabited rather than appropriated. This distinction is not abstract — you can hear it in the choices: which phrases get sampled, how they sit in the mix, what gets treated as texture and what gets treated as the point.
My parents were at those Anjuna parties in the 90s. I play psytrance sets in Delhi basements. It is the same sound and it is completely different. This music carries time inside it.
Chakravyūh and What It Means

Qilla's hundredth release is called Chakravyūh — spinning wheel in Sanskrit, also the name of a military formation from the Mahabharata that surrounds you from all sides. Madhav Shorey describes it as a rebirth, a catapult to the next phase. 35 tracks, 34 artists, 12 countries. On it, Kohra and SHFT produce 'Morjim Mystery' — named for a beach in Goa, built from psytrance architecture, made in Delhi by two producers who grew up in the country whose culture the original genre mined without acknowledgement.

This is what the psytrance revival in India actually is. Not nostalgia. Not ironic distance. Not the European festival circuit's third or fourth round with a sound that never quite went away. It is something more specific: a generation of Indian producers encountering a genre that was made from their cultural material, finding it compelling, and deciding to make it their own on their own terms. The Anjuna parties are over. The question of who gets to say where the music came from is not.

articles.title: Dressing Against the Grain
articles.subtitle: The queer fashion revolution in urban India is not asking for permission
articles.author: Siya Kapoor
articles.lead: India decriminalised homosexuality in 2018. Eight years later, the visual culture that had been building for decades before the law caught up is finally visible enough that everyone else is copying it.

body.text: The popular account of queer visibility in India has a before and an after: September 6, 2018, when the Supreme Court read down Section 377. This account is wrong in its simplicity. The queer communities in Delhi, Mumbai, Bengaluru, and Kolkata who built their visual identities while that law was still on the books did not wait for permission to dress. What they built — through constraint, through the specific creativity that comes from navigating a world designed to exclude you — is what the broader fashion culture is now borrowing.

Saisha Shinde designed the gown that Harnaaz Kaur Sandhu wore to win Miss Universe 2021. She is India's first publicly transgender fashion designer. Her presence at the top of Indian fashion is not a symbol of progress. It is evidence of something that was always there: a strand of Indian fashion culture that understood gender as a design problem rather than a fixed constraint, that had been doing this work in various registers — in the hijra communities, in Lavani performance, in Kaniyan Koothu, in Launda Naach — long before it became a headline.
Fashion is more than a profession for me — it is a survival skill. My language of rebellion is not asking people for acceptance. It is showing them that I am queer, and so is what I wear.
What the Pre-Colonial Record Actually Says

The dominant account of gender in India's past is the Victorian one. The Criminal Tribes Act of 1871, the various colonial morality legislations, the rigidities that were imposed on a culture that already had its own, more complicated relationship to gender and performance. The hijra community predates the colonial period. The Kaniyan Koothu tradition in Tamil Nadu, the Launda Naach in Bihar, the lavani performers of Maharashtra — these traditions survived colonialism and are not footnotes. They are the actual history.

Glorious Luna — non-binary, gay, model, drag performer, co-founder of India's first queer talent management agency — describes fashion as a survival skill before a profession. That framing is not rhetorical. When the choices you make about how to dress are connected to your physical safety and your capacity to move through the world, those choices become highly refined. The visual intelligence that emerges from that refinement is what the rest of fashion downloads without always acknowledging the source.
The Drag Scene as R&D

The drag scene that emerged in Indian metros over the last five years has had an outsized influence on broader street fashion that is rarely credited. Elements that were drag-specific three years ago — extreme proportions, gender-ambiguous silhouettes, traditional Indian garments used in deconstructed ways, heavy jewellery worn with club clothing — are now visible on the street, in the thrift accounts, in the homegrown labels. The drag performers in India's cities are functioning as a research and development operation for a broader visual culture. The broader visual culture is downloading their output at scale.

Siddartha Tytler's post-lockdown show put men in lehengas. The response was international: a 55-year-old man in New York reportedly wanted one purely for the statement. Tytler describes it as blurred binaries. The lehenga is not a woman's garment when a man wears it — it becomes something else entirely, a possibility that was always available in the garment and just needed someone to insist on it.
I'm not dressing to be understood. I'm dressing to be seen. Those are very different things. Understanding takes time. Being seen is immediate.
What Queer Fashion Actually Is

Rayyan — a Muslim transfemme pansexual model and content creator from Mumbai — makes a point that the more visible accounts of queer fashion tend to obscure: only a tiny portion of the queer population is flamboyant. Most people navigate quietly. The drag, the extreme proportions, the gender-defying silhouettes that circulate on social media represent one end of a very wide spectrum.

The more interesting question is not what queer fashion looks like at its most visible. It is what changes when the sensibility — the specific attention to the body, the history of using clothing as communication when other forms of communication were foreclosed, the political sophistication that comes from navigating a world not designed for you — becomes part of how everyone thinks about what they wear. The answer, in 2026, looking at what Indian streetwear actually is, is: quite a lot. The question of credit remains open.

brands.name: KAASAW
brands.desc: Deconstructed silhouettes rooted in Dalit Bahujan identity. Handwoven cotton, raw edges, no apologies.
brands.story: KAASAW began in Dharavi in 2022 when founder Rohini Kamble started reworking surplus fabric into pieces that reflected a Dalit Bahujan identity the mainstream fashion industry had never thought to represent. The name means crow — intelligence, memory, survival.

brands.name: OBLIQUE
brands.desc: Streetwear that takes from temple architecture and returns it as bombers and wide-leg cuts.
brands.story: Founder Dev Sharma spent three years studying Hoysala temple architecture before making his first garment. The rhythm of columns, the logic of proportion — these become the grammar of pattern-cutting.

brands.name: MURMUR
brands.desc: Surplus fabric sourced from garment factories. Remixed into something new.
brands.story: MURMUR was founded on a simple premise: Delhi's garment industry produces more fabric than it uses. The constraint of found material produced better design decisions than a blank canvas.

brands.name: NOIR DUST
brands.desc: Rave-ready clubwear that works on a beach at 4am.
brands.story: NOIR DUST was designed for a specific environment: the outdoor party at the end of a long night. The clothes are built around that constraint with unusual precision.

brands.name: BHUVI
brands.desc: Traditional craft techniques — block print, resist dye — worn differently.
brands.story: BHUVI works directly with block printing and resist dye artisans in Rajasthan. Founder Meera Sharma created a market for craft practiced for generations.

brands.name: WILT
brands.desc: Slow fashion. Distressed textures, earth tones.
brands.story: WILT makes clothes meant to degrade intentionally — through a design philosophy that treats the garment's lifespan as part of its aesthetic.

brands.name: NEEDLE
brands.desc: Every piece numbered. Every season a limited print run.
brands.story: NEEDLE treats each season as a zine: finite print run, a specific number, a collector's logic applied to clothing. When the run is sold, it's gone.

brands.name: FLORA
brands.story: FLORA is inseparable from Kolkata — its humidity, its faded grandeur, its specific relationship with colonial history. Every piece processes something.
